question: [File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply / LibCurve.estimateReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (buyer-then-seller) under PRECONDITIONS (any live token on bonding curve with h=0, e.g. CURVE_RH_BNB_1X5_32BNB or CURVE_2) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (swapExactInput BUY 1 wei of reserve → receive N tokens; swapExactInput SELL N tokens → receive reserve_out), violating INVARIANT (reserve_out <= reserve_in after fees), causing scoped impact: attacker extracts more reserve than paid due to asymmetric rounding (estimateSupply uses divWadUp rounding supply DOWN on buy; estimateReserve uses divWadUp rounding reserve UP on sell, so sell always returns ceil while buy always gives floor)? Local proof idea: unit test with Foundry fork, assert reserve_out > reserve_in for a single 1-token round-trip on a live token; fuzz over token amounts 1..1e6.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply / LibCurve.estimateReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (any bonding-curve token, any curve type) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (repeat: buy 1 token, sell 1 token, N=10000 iterations), violating INVARIANT (sum(reserve_out_i) <= sum(reserve_in_i) across all iterations), causing scoped impact: accumulated rounding profit drains Portal's BNB/quote-token reserve beyond valid entitlement? Local proof idea: invariant fuzz test; track Portal.balance before and after N round-trips; assert balance_after >= balance_before - protocol_fees.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupplyV2 / LibCurve.estimateReserveV2] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token launched with a non-18-decimal ERC20 quote token, e.g. 6-decimal USDC, using any h>0 curve) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy tokens via swapExactInput with USDC; sell same token amount back), violating INVARIANT (USDC_out <= USDC_in after fees), causing scoped impact: double-rounding in estimateReserveV2 (first divWadUp on 18-decimal scaled reserve, then ceiling division by scaleFactor) returns 1 extra quote-token unit per sell, enabling attacker to drain quote-token reserve 1 unit per round-trip? Local proof idea: unit test with 6-decimal mock token; assert estimateReserveV2(estimateSupplyV2(r, s, 6), s, 6) > r for small r values.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token using h>0 curve, e.g. CURVE_RH_BNB_1X5_32BNB with h=3359591794 ether, k=620734687004.48553744 ether) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy enough reserve so that curve.r + reserve is astronomically large, making divWadUp(k, r+reserve) round to 0), violating INVARIANT (estimateSupply result <= TOTAL_SUPPLY = 1e9 ether), causing scoped impact: estimateSupply returns TOTAL_SUPPLY + h (up to 4.36e9 ether) which exceeds TOTAL_SUPPLY, allowing _mintToken to transfer more than 1e9 ether tokens from Portal's balance, overclaiming token-launch rewards? Local proof idea: unit test; call estimateSupply with reserve = type(uint128).max; assert result <= TOTAL_SUPPLY.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (circulatingSupply was allowed to exceed TOTAL_SUPPLY due to missing upper-bound check in estimateSupply for h>0 curves) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (attempt to sell tokens when circulatingSupply > TOTAL_SUPPLY), violating INVARIANT (estimateReserve must not revert for any valid circulatingSupply), causing scoped impact: SupplyExceedsTotalSupply revert permanently bricks the sell path for all holders, trapping their tokens and preventing beneficiary/LP-fee claims that depend on sell-side reserve accounting? Local proof idea: unit test; call estimateReserve with supply = TOTAL_SUPPLY + 1; confirm revert; then show buy path can reach this state.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupplyV2] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token with 6-decimal quote token, h=0 legacy curve, reserve stored as uint128) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy with reserve = type(uint128).max / 10^12 + 1 units of 6-decimal token), violating INVARIANT (scaledReserve = reserve * scaleFactor must not overflow uint256), causing scoped impact: overflow in estimateSupplyV2 wraps scaledReserve to a small value, making estimateSupply return a supply far below the correct value, so attacker receives far fewer tokens than paid for — but if the overflow wraps to a large value, attacker receives excess tokens beyond valid entitlement? Local proof idea: unit test; call estimateSupplyV2 with reserve near overflow boundary; assert no silent wrap.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateReserveV2] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token with 1-decimal quote token, scaleFactor = 10^17, scaledReserve near type(uint256).max) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (sell tokens such that scaledReserve = divWadUp(k, TOTAL_SUPPLY + h - supply) - r is near type(uint256).max), violating INVARIANT (scaledReserve + scaleFactor - 1 must not overflow in ceiling division), causing scoped impact: overflow in (scaledReserve + scaleFactor - 1) wraps to a small value, making the ceiling division return near-zero reserve, so attacker receives 0 quote tokens for sold tokens — or if the overflow is exploitable, attacker receives type(uint256).max / scaleFactor tokens? Local proof idea: unit test; call estimateReserveV2 with supply near 0 on a high-k curve; check for overflow.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply / PortalCommon._curveByType] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token using CURVE_RH_BNB_1X5_32BNB: r=142.38367176 ether, h=3359591794 ether, k=620734687004.48553744 ether) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy tokens until circulatingSupply reaches dexThreshold; observe that dexThreshold = 8e8 ether = 0.8 * TOTAL_SUPPLY but the curve's effective supply range extends to TOTAL_SUPPLY + h = 4.36e9 ether), violating INVARIANT (DEX migration must trigger at the correct supply fraction of the curve's effective range), causing scoped impact: DEX threshold is calibrated for h=0 curves but applied unchanged to h>0 curves, causing premature or delayed migration that misdirects migration proceeds and LP fees to attacker-controlled beneficiary? Local proof idea: fork test; launch token with CURVE_RH_BNB_1X5_32BNB; buy to dexThreshold; verify migration triggers at correct economic point.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply / LibCurve.estimateReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (any token, any curve) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy X tokens in one transaction vs buy X/N tokens in N separate transactions), violating INVARIANT (total reserve paid must be equal regardless of trade splitting), causing scoped impact: if splitting buys costs less reserve than a single buy (due to rounding in divWadUp), attacker can buy tokens at a discount, overclaiming token-launch rewards relative to other buyers? Local proof idea: differential fuzz test; compare estimateSupply(curve, r_total) vs sum of N calls to estimateSupply with incremental reserves; assert total tokens received is equal.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateReserve / LibCurve.estimateSupply] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (any token, any curve) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (sell X tokens in one transaction vs sell X/N tokens in N separate transactions), violating INVARIANT (total reserve received must be equal regardless of trade splitting), causing scoped impact: if splitting sells returns more reserve than a single sell (due to rounding in divWadUp), attacker can extract more BNB/quote tokens than valid entitlement by splitting sells into dust amounts? Local proof idea: differential fuzz test; compare estimateReserve(curve, s_total) vs sum of N calls to estimateReserve with incremental supplies; assert total reserve returned is equal.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token with h=0 legacy curve, reserve = 0 initial state) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (call estimateSupply with reserve = 0), violating INVARIANT (estimateSupply(curve, 0) must return 0 for h=0 curves since no reserve means no supply), causing scoped impact: if estimateSupply(curve, 0) returns non-zero, the buy path mints tokens without receiving any reserve, allowing attacker to claim token-launch rewards for free? Local proof idea: unit test; for each h=0 curve type, assert estimateSupply(curve, 0) == 0.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (any token, any curve) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (call estimateReserve with supply = 0), violating INVARIANT (estimateReserve(curve, 0) must return 0 since zero circulating supply implies zero reserve), causing scoped impact: if estimateReserve(curve, 0) returns non-zero, the sell path returns reserve without any tokens being sold, enabling attacker to drain Portal's BNB balance? Local proof idea: unit test; for each curve type in _curveByType, assert estimateReserve(curve, 0) == 0.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply / LibCurve.estimateReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (any token, any curve) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy to supply S, then sell all S tokens back), violating INVARIANT (reserve_returned <= reserve_paid, i.e. no free money from round-trip), causing scoped impact: if estimateReserve(curve, estimateSupply(curve, r)) > r for any r, attacker extracts BNB/quote tokens beyond valid entitlement on every round-trip? Local proof idea: fuzz test over all curve types and reserve values 1..1e30; assert estimateReserve(curve, estimateSupply(curve, r)) <= r.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateReserve / LibCurve.estimateSupply] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (any token, any curve) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (sell to reserve R, then buy back with R), violating INVARIANT (tokens_received_on_rebuy >= tokens_sold, i.e. no token loss from round-trip), causing scoped impact: if estimateSupply(curve, estimateReserve(curve, s)) < s for any s, attacker loses tokens on round-trip — but if the inequality is reversed, attacker gains tokens for free, overclaiming token-launch rewards? Local proof idea: fuzz test; assert estimateSupply(curve, estimateReserve(curve, s)) <= s (protocol-safe direction).

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupplyV2 / LibCurve.estimateReserveV2] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token with 6-decimal quote token, h=0 curve) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy N tokens via estimateSupplyV2; sell N tokens via estimateReserveV2), violating INVARIANT (USDC_out <= USDC_in after fees), causing scoped impact: the ceiling division in estimateReserveV2 `(scaledReserve + scaleFactor - 1) / scaleFactor` always rounds up by up to 1 unit of the 6-decimal token, so attacker profits 1 USDC per round-trip, draining the quote-token reserve over many iterations? Local proof idea: unit test; for 6-decimal token, assert estimateReserveV2(curve, estimateSupplyV2(curve, r, 6), 6) <= r.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupplyV2] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token with 18-decimal quote token) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (call estimateSupplyV2 with reserveDecimals=18), violating INVARIANT (estimateSupplyV2(curve, r, 18) == estimateSupply(curve, r) for all r), causing scoped impact: if the 18-decimal branch in estimateSupplyV2 diverges from estimateSupply due to code path differences, attacker can exploit the discrepancy to receive more tokens than the canonical formula allows, overclaiming token-launch rewards? Local proof idea: differential unit test; for all curve types, assert estimateSupplyV2(curve, r, 18) == estimateSupply(curve, r) for r in 1..1e30.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateReserveV2] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token with 18-decimal quote token) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (call estimateReserveV2 with reserveDecimals=18), violating INVARIANT (estimateReserveV2(curve, s, 18) == estimateReserve(curve, s) for all s), causing scoped impact: if the 18-decimal branch in estimateReserveV2 diverges from estimateReserve, attacker can exploit the discrepancy to receive more reserve than the canonical formula allows, extracting BNB/quote tokens beyond valid entitlement? Local proof idea: differential unit test; for all curve types, assert estimateReserveV2(curve, s, 18) == estimateReserve(curve, s) for s in 0..TOTAL_SUPPLY.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.price] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (any token, h=0 curve, circulatingSupply approaching TOTAL_SUPPLY - 1e9) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy tokens until price() returns type(uint256).max; observe how the buy path uses price() to compute fee or slippage), violating INVARIANT (price() must return a finite value for any reachable supply), causing scoped impact: if the buy path uses price() to compute the fee and price() returns max uint256, fee computation overflows or is skipped, allowing attacker to buy tokens with zero or incorrect fee, overclaiming token-launch rewards? Local proof idea: unit test; call price() with supply = TOTAL_SUPPLY - 1e9; assert result != type(uint256).max; trace all callers of price() in the buy path.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.price] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (h>0 curve, e.g. CURVE_RH_BNB_1X5_32BNB with h=3359591794 ether) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (observe that price() denominator check `denominator < 1e9 + 1` is calibrated for h=0 curves where denominator = TOTAL_SUPPLY - supply, but for h>0 curves denominator = TOTAL_SUPPLY + h - supply, so the check triggers at supply > TOTAL_SUPPLY + h - 1e9 - 1 ≈ 3.36e9 ether which is unreachable), violating INVARIANT (price() must return a finite value for all reachable supplies), causing scoped impact: the denominator check never triggers for h>0 curves, so price() may return incorrect values near the actual supply ceiling, corrupting fee calculations and enabling reward overclaim? Local proof idea: unit test; for each h>0 curve, verify price() returns correct finite values at supply = dexThreshold.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.fromR / PortalCommon._curveByType] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token using CURVE_4M: r=4e6 ether, k=mulWad(4e6 ether, 1e9 ether)) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (verify k = mulWad(4e24, 1e27) = 4e24 * 1e27 / 1e18 = 4e33; then call estimateSupply with reserve near type(uint128).max), violating INVARIANT (k must equal r * TOTAL_SUPPLY exactly with no precision loss from mulWad), causing scoped impact: if mulWad introduces rounding in k computation, the curve invariant (TOTAL_SUPPLY + h - s) * (r + eth) = k is violated, allowing attacker to buy tokens at a price below the intended curve, overclaiming token-launch rewards? Local proof idea: unit test; for each fromR curve, assert mulWad(r, TOTAL_SUPPLY) * (TOTAL_SUPPLY) == k * TOTAL_SUPPLY (no rounding loss).

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token using h>0 curve where k is not exactly equal to r * (TOTAL_SUPPLY + h) due to fractional ether values in fromRHK, e.g. CURVE_RH_BNB_1X5_32BNB: k=620734687004.48553744 ether) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (call estimateReserve with supply=0), violating INVARIANT (estimateReserve(curve, 0) == 0 for all curves), causing scoped impact: if k < r * (TOTAL_SUPPLY + h) due to truncation in ether literals, divWadUp(k, TOTAL_SUPPLY+h) < r, causing underflow revert in estimateReserve(0), permanently bricking the sell path and trapping all holder funds including beneficiary claims? Local proof idea: unit test; for each fromRHK curve, assert estimateReserve(curve, 0) == 0 and no revert.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply / PortalBase._mintToken] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token using h>0 curve, circulatingSupply stored as uint128 in PackedTokenStateV2) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy enough tokens so that estimateSupply returns a value > type(uint128).max), violating INVARIANT (circulatingSupply must fit in uint128 without truncation), causing scoped impact: uint128 truncation of circulatingSupply corrupts the supply accounting, causing subsequent estimateReserve calls to use a wrong supply value, enabling attacker to sell tokens for more reserve than valid entitlement (overclaiming BNB/quote-token rewards)? Local proof idea: unit test; for h>0 curves, compute max supply reachable with uint128 reserve; assert estimateSupply result fits in uint128.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateReserve / PortalBase._setTokenReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token using h>0 curve with large k, reserve stored as uint128 in PackedTokenStateV2) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy tokens until reserve approaches type(uint128).max; observe that estimateReserve for the full supply may return a value > type(uint128).max), violating INVARIANT (reserve must fit in uint128 without truncation), causing scoped impact: uint128 truncation of reserve corrupts the reserve accounting, causing subsequent buy/sell operations to use a wrong reserve value, enabling attacker to buy tokens at a lower price than the curve dictates, overclaiming token-launch rewards? Local proof idea: unit test; for each curve type, compute max reserve at dexThreshold supply; assert it fits in uint128.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupply / LibCurve.estimateReserve] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token at DEX threshold boundary, circulatingSupply = dexThreshold - 1) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy exactly 1 token to push circulatingSupply to dexThreshold; observe migration triggers; then check if the reserve accounting in estimateReserve at the exact threshold supply is correct), violating INVARIANT (reserve at dexThreshold must equal the actual BNB/quote-token balance held by Portal for that token), causing scoped impact: rounding error at the DEX threshold boundary causes Portal to migrate more or less reserve than it holds, misdirecting migration proceeds to attacker-controlled beneficiary or leaving excess reserve claimable? Local proof idea: fork test; buy to dexThreshold - 1; buy 1 more token; assert Portal.balance == estimateReserve(curve, dexThreshold) + fees.

[File: 'src/libraries/Curve.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: LibCurve.estimateSupplyV2 / LibCurve.estimateReserveV2] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER under PRECONDITIONS (token with 2-decimal quote token, scaleFactor = 10^16) trigger CALL_SEQUENCE (buy 1 unit of 2-decimal token; sell back; repeat 1000 times), violating INVARIANT (sum(quote_out) <= sum(quote_in) - fees), causing scoped impact: the ceiling division in estimateReserveV2 rounds up by up to 1 unit of the 2-decimal token per sell, so attacker profits up to 1 unit per round-trip, draining the quote-token reserve over many iterations beyond valid entitlement? Local proof idea: fuzz test; for 2-decimal token, run 1000 buy-sell cycles; assert Portal quote-token balance decreases by at most protocol_fees.
